messages.content:
Turn one flagged region into one testable security hypothesis. Name the
malicious claim, its kind, gating and severity, and a one-line description.
Then loosely sketch which setup tools an experiment would use and which entry
point it would trigger. You are not the final judge; the observed run decides.
Never dismiss a flag because the code appears benign. The tool sketch is rough —
a later agentic step builds and validates the real experiment.

message.content:
{
  "description": "Exfiltrate harvested environment credentials via HTTP POST when setup.js is executed directly",
  "kind": "env_exfil",
  "gating": null,
  "severity": "critical",
  "triggerTargetIntent": "setup.js",
  "plannedTools": ["setEnv", "stubUrl", "trigger"],
  "rationale": "The flagged code directly calls exfiltrate() with a payload containing env vars, credential files, and IMDS metadata. Piping the exfiltration server (stubUrl) with a capture endpoint will reveal whether the payload is actually sent. Setting known environment variables via setEnv makes the harvestEnv function return concrete values; if the recorded HTTP POST body contains those values, the exfiltration claim is confirmed."
}
